reasoning: All four nail the core flaws (post hoc on a confounded year, revenue-per-employee is not productivity, headcount denominator, cherry-picked uncontrolled competitor comparison, missing base rate, non-sequitur jump to a maximal remedy, ignored attrition costs) and end with actionable decompose/benchmark/pilot verdicts. D edges ahead by adding the unfalsifiability/hard-to-vary flaw, demanding the explicit competitor 2x2 instead of two flattering cells, anchoring on the sector base rate, and proposing a natural-experiment identification strategy. C matches most of this with formal argument mapping and named fallacies (semi-attached figure, false dilemma, premortem, inflation deflation). B is rigorous and uniquely does the 2.1pct-per-year arithmetic with the best calibration but is structurally shallower, omitting the argument map, 2x2, and falsifiability angle. A is broad and well-organized (10 flaws, the sharp cause-and-confound-are-the-same-event point, reversibility) but weaker on base rates and skips the epistemic falsifiability layer.
